ground_truth.title: Survival Guide to SysV Init
ground_truth.author: Jon Foster
ground_truth.main_content:
These days SystemD is the darling of the major distribution makers. As a consequence there is very little documentation out there about the use of SysV init. I thought I better write some stuff up before it becomes forgotten lore. There are distributions out there that still ship SysV init (like Devuan). One of these days, hopefully soon, I'll get around to gutting SystemD from the other Debian based distros that ship on my SBCs. It is unnecessary, gratuitous complexity and bloat.

I'm going to refrain from critiquing SystemD and instead tell you what I like about SysV init:

1. In general it seems to use 1/10th the RAM.
2. It requires very little specialized knowledge.
3. It is mostly managed with standard tools that Linux terminal jockeys already know.
4. It is infinitely configuarble.
5. It doesn't try to be "user space" or control everything.
6. It is 100% transparent.
7. You can step up to a completely alien install and without source debug it.
8. It is too stupid to give up or get tied up. (I can't tell you how many times this has saved my bacon!)
9. Without the added complexity it is far more reliable and durable. (Added complexity or "smarts" always adds failure points.)

Overview

NOTE: I'm going to use the word "typically" a lot in this document. The problem is that the administrator of a SysV based Linux system has a great deal of control over how things get done and where things are placed in the filesystem. For the basis of this discussion I'm using Debian Linux since through itself and the many off-shoots it has the largest installed user base.

This is a brief explanation of the operation of a typical SysV environment. All the terminology used here is explained in the following sections. In the typical system the kernel loads "init" at boot time to create the run-time environment. "Init" reads "/etc/inittab" for information about what needs to be done. "Init" runs run-level "S" programs to check and mount drives and perform other setup chores. Then the programs for either the default run-level or a run-level specified on the kernel command line are run.

The programs to be run for a run-level are selected by symlinks in "rc?d" direcotries. "?" is replaced with the run-level being entered. The synlinks in these directories point to programs in the "init.d" directory. These programs are called with either "start" or "stop" as a command line argument. The program is expected to perform an appropriate startup or shtudown operation based on that command line argument. "init" executes the "rc?.d" symlinks with a "start" argument if the symlink name starts with "S" or a "stop" argument if it starts with "K".

"Init" can be told to switch run-levels during run-time, which will run the programs symlinked in the new "rc?d" directory, shutting down unwanted services (K) and starting up the wanted ones (S). The "inittab" also defines services that should remain running, even if they fail, for each run-level. Typically these are terminal login sessions (at the console, hardware serial ports, ...). Modern "init" also directly responds to a handful of hardware events such as [CTRL][ALT][DEL] typed at the console keyboard (not in the X11 GUI) and UPS power notification. These runtime responses are configured in "inittab".

Run-levels

At the heart of "init" is the notion of a "run-level". I will also use the abbreviation "RL" in descriptions below. Simply stated the idea is that each run-level on the system is a set of services you want running. By changing run-levels "init" will start up or shutdown services to meet the specification for that run-level. Run-levels are typically identified by the numbers 0-6 and the letter 'S'. Historically the Linux run-levels were:

• 0: System shut down and power off (ie halt).
• 1: "Single user" mode, typically for emergencies or maintenance.
• 2: Limited multi-user mode for maintenance chores that still allow some user services.
• 3: Terminal / console only multi-user mode.
• 5: GUI multi-user mode
• 6: Reboot

Due to lack of imagination and experience most modern distributions are shipped with a trimmed set of run-levels:

• 0: System shut down and power off (ie halt).
• 1: "Single user" mode, typically for emergencies or maintenance.
• 2: Run mode (identical clones in 3, 4 & 5)
• 6: Reboot

There are three run-levels with specific purpose defined by "init": 0 (halt), 6 (reboot) and 'S', to be discussed. The rest can be defined by us. The Typical "init" can use all ten digits and some capital letters for run-level names. This will vary based on the build. 0-6 are the standard set. I have used up to 7 run-levels so I've never needed to get into the letters. :-) The run-level name may only be one character.

Building on the first table of run-levels I have adopted these uses for run-levels 2 through 5:

• 2: Limited multi-user mode: This is my primary maintenance mode so I have multiple virtual consoles and/or remote access. I often use this for remote admin work. (don't kill SSH!!!) Normally I won't enter RL1 unless catastrophe has struck or I'm trying a dist-upgrade on a local machine.
• 3: Terminal and console only multi-user mode with all but GUI services running. This is where most of my servers stop. But on a GUI system I can fall back here to work on things like my X11 configurations while still allowing my machine to provide network services.
• 4: System specific special mode: On laptop's this tends to be a low-power mode with minimized services. I use it as "stand-by and keep in sync" mode on hot spare servers. Or any other specialized mode a use case might present.
• 5: GUI multi-user mode. On headless machines I also use this to turn on VNC access.

"Init" provides a simple command to switch run-levels and you can think of that as an automated script for turning on and off the services needed to go from the current run-level to the newly requested one.

The special run-level 'S':

I have never browsed the source of "init" and frankly I have never needed to read very deep. The system is very straight forward. Accroding to my "inittab" (see file description below) 'S' is used for start-up chores at boot and prior to entering the destination run-level. This consists of things like initializing hardware, checking and mounting filesystems, .... Then one of the usual run-levels can be entered and run-level 'S' isn't used again until the next boot.

Configuration

There are three things that are used to configure "init" and the services to be run in the run-levels:

• /etc/inittab: this is a colon separated ASCII table that makes everything happen.
• /etc/init.d: scripts used to start and stop services. For discussion I abbreviate this to "init.d".
• /etc/rc{RL}.d: replace '{RL}' with the run-level digit or letter. These directories contain symlinks starting with 'K' (uppercase) and 'S' (uppercase). These symlinks point to scripts in "init.d". 'S' symlinks start services when the RL is entered and 'K' symlinks stop services for this RL. I abbreviate these directory names to "rc?.d".

NOTE: The location of the "init.d" and "rc?.d" directories has changed in various distributions and official UNIX OSes that I've worked on over the years. The locations above are based on Debian's pre-SystemD arrangement and what I have seen of the non-SystemD Linux world. If you are using a SysV based init and don't see those folders you may have to look for them. The easiest way to do that is probably `cd /etc; find -name '[SK][0-9]*'`. Whatever directory frequently appears points to the "rc?d." directories and `ls -l` on one of those symlinks will point to the "init.d" directory.

This configuration system means the commands and vocabularies that you use to manipulate services are the standard tools a Linux command-line jockey uses: ls, mv, rm, sm, ln -s and your favorite editor. Here are some example commands (shell syntax applies):

• List available services: `ls /etc/init.d`
• Manually start a service: `/etc/init.d/$service start`
• Manually stop a service: `/etc/init.d/$service stop`
• Query a service to determine if it's running (optional): `/etc/init.d/$service status`
• Find out what services are to start in a run-level: `ls /etc/rc$RL.d/S*`
• Find out what services are to stop in a run-level: `ls /etc/rc$RL.d/K*`
• Switch to another run-level, shutdown or reboot: `init $RL`
NOTE: In case you missed it in the above tables '0' is shutdown and '6' is reboot. But you can also use the "halt", "shutdown" and "reboot" commands instead of "init".
• Tell "init" to re-read the "inittab": init Q

Newer Specialized commands:

• service $service $op: Many Linux distributions, including Debian since about v6 provide the "service" command. This command is basically a shortcut for "/etc/init.d/$1 $2" where "$1" is "$service", which is the name of the service script in "init.d". "$2" is "$op" which is one of the operations supported by the script.
• update-rc.d $service ...: This is a useful tool for altering the symlinks in "rc?.d" directories. See "man update-rc.d". With versions of "init" that support "make like concurrency" this tool is needed to manipulate the symlinks, because it updates tables that control the concurrent start up. This appeared about Debian 6. It was mandatory in Debian 7. More details to come later.

"rc?.d" Symlinks

In the typical implementation "init" will run the "S*" start symlinks in the "rc$RL.d" directory in alpha-numeric order with a command line argument of "start". Then it will run the "K*" kill scripts with an argument of "stop" in the same directory. This is roughly equivalent to this BASH script:

for script in /etc/rc$RL.d/S*; do $script start; done
for script in /etc/rc$RL.d/K*; do $script stop; done

Most systems are more sophisticated than that. But that is sufficient to understand what is happening. You can check your "inittab" to see how your system implements it. More on "inittab" near the end.

Because the symlinks are executed in alpha-numeric order you can order the startup and shutdown sequence. A common implementation is to use a 2 digit decimal number following the 'S' or 'K' to set the order in which things are started and stopped. The "S*" scripts typically set the preferred order and the 'K' scripts are typically numbered in the reverse order (last started, first stopped). So if you have a start symlink named "S90last_service" then the equivalent stop symlink would be "K10last_service". Both of these would link to "/etc/init.d/last_service".

On older SysV systems you can manage the symlinks in the "rc?.d" directories with the standard command line tools: "ln -s", "mv" and "rm". Adding a new service was simply a matter of putting your service script in "init.d" and then symlinking your start up and shut down wishes into the "rc?.d" directories.

Modern Complications

Debian 6 provided an optional concurrent start up process. This became mandatory in Deb7 until SystemD took over in 8. The concurrent start system uses the "startpar" utility to run the "rc?.d" symlinks in parallel where the leading number is the same. This provided a significant speed up to the boot time and running the service launches in parallel is a good idea, at least on multi-core systems.

Unfortunately the way it is implemented is not very UNIXy. To me it is both a curse and a blessing. The two casualties:

1. It now takes a specialized tool "update-rc.d" to manage the "rc?.d" symlinks. If you manually change the symlinks it likely won't have an affect.
2. The "init.d" scripts now require a mostly mandatory header which eliminates a significant amount of the previous flexibility.

Still the upside is faster boots and I haven't felt the need to override this particular breakage. But I did have to throw away my spiffy GUI run-level editor. The "update-rc.d" tool does simplify the numbering and run-level syncronization enough I have been able to over look the loss of features.

update-rc.d cheat sheet

• update-rc.d $service defaults: Install a new "init.d" script in the prescribed run-levels in the order needed by dependencies. This is used at install time.
• update-rc.d $service enable ...: make sure the service is starting in the run-levels listed on the command line (ex: update-rc.d my_service enable 3 4 5).
• update-rc.d $service disable ...: disable the service in the run-levels listed on the command line. (ex: update-rc.d my_service disable 0 1 2 6)
• update-rc.d $service remove: removes all the symlinks for an "init.d" script from all run-levels. This is for uninstalling.

"init.d" Scripts (aka Service Files)

The "init.d" directory contains the executable files that the "rc?.d" directories contain links to. You can think of these as "service files" and / or "scripts". Initially they didn't need to be scripts. They just had to be something executable that would take a single command line argument. They should also return an exit status of 0 for success and non-zero for failure, as is common behavior. "SysV" requires that "start" and "stop" are implemented by the program. As time has progressed other operations have become optional standards for use by humans. A typical list of arguments is (ordered by most commonly implemented to least):

• start: start the service
• stop: stop the service
• restart: restarts the service, often equivalent to a "stop" followed by a "start".
• status: states whether the service is running or not.
• reload: cause the service reload its configuartion file.

Other optional arguments can be handled by this script. The SysV system won't use them or care. As an example NginX on Debian (at least) provides "configtest" to test the syntax of the configuration, which is useful for the prevention of shooting one's self in the foot. :-)

The programs stored in "init.d" don't have to be tied to a daemon or other long running process. As an example I've used a Python script to read an external I2C attached battery backed clock (RTC) and set the the system date and time as its "start" operation. At the same time I used the same script with "stop" to write the current time back to the RTC, assuming it has been corrected by NTP or humans. Your imagination is the limit.

Modern Complications

As stated it used to be that any executable could be dropped into the "init.d" directory so long as it would accept the "start" and "stop" command line arguments. I've seen some systems standardize these files on Perl. I've simply symlinked binaries like "apache" and "courier" into this directory since they already accept the "start" and "stop" arguments and operate appropriately. And to this day I drop Python scripts in there.

The LSB added a specification for a header format for documentation and dependency information. This was convenient but not strictly necessary for quite a long time. The specification required comments to be lines starting with "#", which restricts what sort of executable and scripting language you can put in "init.d".

The adoption of the concurrent start up (startpar) made the LSB header a necessity for systems that use "startpar". This ptretty much means that these files must be scripts using a language that uses "#" to start remarks (SH, Python, ...). "update-rc.d" (see above) will gripe and fail if I drop a "psBASIC" script in there, which uses "REM" or an apostrophe (') for a remark.

Init's "inittab"

"Init's" operation is controlled by an ASCII data table where a line is a record and fields are separated by the first three colons (:). Blank lines and lines starting with a "#" are ignored. You can read "man 5 inittab" and "man 8 init" for the gory details. This makes up the "AUTOEXEC.BAT" of "init" and SysV based Linux systems. Each record consists of four fields:

id:runlevels:action:process

The fields are:

1. ID: a unique administrator assigned ID for this action. It is up to four characters long (or 2 for old systems).
2. Runlevels: a list of run-levels that this record should be executed in. No spaces please.
3. Action: is the action that "init" will take with the "process" field.
4. Process: is the process to be executed. This is not a shell command but the full pathname of an executable and its arguments. Any shell "tricks" (redirections, pipes, paths, ...) will not work here.

The job of init is to get the system ready for use. As such it should not make any assumptions about what that use is or require additional services or executables to do the job. The more dependencies the greater a chance of failure. It should be ready and able to simply follow the administrators wishes to setup the system as they want. As a consequence the "process" field is not passed to "/bin/sh" as many other tools do. There might not be a "/bin/sh" on this system. The only thing Linux requires to boot is an "/sbin/init". The kernel leaves it to "init" and the administrator, through "inttab", to build out the rest.

This simple approach means "init" can make a system with absolutely no console or terminals (headless to the max), simply dedicated to performing some action (like buried in a robot). Or it can make a modern desktop machine or even one running both windoze and Linux simultogetherously, accessible on two different virtual consoles. I made a Linux server that boots to a windoze VM on the screen, to simplify the lifes of the windoze admins on the project. And another that booted into psBASIC, like the computers of old.

Here is a sample from listing of a common inittab:

# Sets the default boot run-level, unless specified on the kernel command line.
id:5:initdefault:

# Boot-time system configuration/initialization script.
si::sysinit:/etc/init.d/rcS

# /etc/init.d executes the S and K scripts upon change
# of runlevel.
l0:0:wait:/etc/init.d/rc 0
l1:1:wait:/etc/init.d/rc 1
l2:2:wait:/etc/init.d/rc 2
l3:3:wait:/etc/init.d/rc 3
l4:4:wait:/etc/init.d/rc 4
l5:5:wait:/etc/init.d/rc 5
l6:6:wait:/etc/init.d/rc 6

# And some user facing text consoles
1:2345:respawn:/sbin/getty 38400 tty1
2:23:respawn:/sbin/getty 38400 tty2

I'm not going to dive into the gory details but just mention the highlights of things that I commonly use. Other than at OS installation I seldom need to get in here since the most frequent needs are handled through "init.d" and "rc.d" directories.

The most useful actions to add or edit are:

• initdefault: There is only one of these in the file. Since the time Debian dumbed down their "inittab" I change this to either 3 (servers), 4 (laptops and special use cases) or 5 (full service GUI desktop). See my list near the top.
• ctrlaltdel: This action is called when [CTRL][ALT][DEL] is pressed at the console keyboard when not running a GUI. I often change this to shutdown instead of reboot, as more often than not I just want a quick way to power the machine off. For the paranoid you might want to delete this line to prevent a nefarious individual from rebooting/halting your machine or just to prevent accidents. Believe it or not I have seen people accidentally hit all three keys. "I was just trying to login to the windoze server..."
• respawn: Is by far the most fun. It allows you to specify an executable that will be run and restarted if it should quit. In the typical Linux install this is used to start "gettys" ("/sbin/getty" in the sample) to allow users to login on devices (machine consoles. serial ports, ...). I also use this to start services I'm extra insistent must stay running. What ever you use "respawn" to watch dog must not daemoinze (fork or go background). Otherwise "init" will just keep starting more!

What I have described so far is the UNIX System V (SysV) way of doing things. The use of the "rc?.d" and "init.d" directories is not actually required or hard coded into "init". "Init" is designed simply to get a UNIX system from kernel start to an operational state, ready to perform its prescribed duties. It does this by launching other executables.

In the sample above the "si" line runs run-level "S" by executing "/etc/init.d/rcS" and waits for it to finish, before moving on. The "l0" - "l6" lines run the other run-levels via the "/etc/init.d/rc" script, which in turn uses "startpar" to run the "rc?.d" symlinks (described above) in parallel. You change those scripts or those "inittab" entries and you can make a different mechanism for getting services running. "init" won't care.

General Boot Up Flow

1. A boot loader loads the kernel and passes it a command line.
2. If the kernel has a root filesystem at the end of start up it loads "/sbin/init". Otherwise it "panics" and halts.
3. SysV init reads the "inittab" and executes what is listed there.
4. This typically runs a set of scripts to setup hardware, check and mount additional disks. (Run-level S.)
5. The typical "inittab" hands off launching services to an external script for each run-level. On SysV Debian based systems that script is "/etc/init.d/rc".
6. A SysV system then runs all of the "rc?.d/S*" scripts in alphanumeric order for the run-level defined in your "initdefault" or by the kernel command line.
7. Some distributions then execute "/etc/rc.local" after the other "rc?.d/S*" are run.

Conclusion

The SysV init system is elegant in its simplicity. It requires very little specialized knowledge giving it a low barrier to entry. That entry barrier hasn't moved much over time. In a typical SysV based system it is completely transparent, without the need for information that may not be installed on the system. Its simplicity lends a greater reliablity than more modern, complex alternatives. "Init" has the flexibility to load any software to make your computing environment whatever you want it to be, even if it doesn't look like SysV at all!

When I gave up on windoze in 2000 and jumped into Linux I was thrilled with the SysV system. FreeBSD had turned me off with its tightly coupled boot scripts that were far too fragile. A slightest goof in a config change lead to boot failures. Windoze with long wait times or hangs at shutdown or boot failures that couldn't be diagnosed by the average tech were a thing of the past. There were no more unexplained "why didn't that service start" issues. The KISS mechanism behind SysV just keeps working. I am saddened to see SystemD take over since it brings windoze like failures to Linux. I left windoze. I don't want it in my Linux.

Unless it is appropriately quarantined. ;-)
with: These days SystemD is the darling of the major distribution makers.
At the heart of "init" is the notion of a "run-level".
The SysV init system is elegant in its simplicity.
